message.content: hey claude. read phase-0-prompt.md and deployment-handoff.md and start implementation. we also have skills you should call. save the chat history and skill calls in a folder inside this project

attachment.content:
- webide-aspnet-prod: Use this skill when editing WebIde.Frontend/Program.cs, AuthController, ExecutionHub, SubmissionController, RedisSubscriptionService, or anything auth/SignalR/middleware-related. It encodes the production middleware order, DataProtection-on-Redis (without which every deploy logs every user out), cookie policy, antiforgery for JSON POST, and rate limiter config from §10 of docs/deployment-handoff.md.
- webide-deploy: Use this skill when working in .github/workflows/ (ci.yml, deploy.yml, sandbox.yml) or anything touching the deploy pipeline, EF migrations, blue-green rollback, or registry tagging. It encodes the §8 blue-green correctness fix (the :latest gotcha) and the §10a migration discipline from docs/deployment-handoff.md.
- webide-sandbox: Use this skill when writing or modifying any sandbox-related file in WebIde — sandbox Dockerfiles (sandbox/*.Dockerfile), wrapper scripts (sandbox/*.sh), SandboxOrchestrator, or SubmissionEvaluator. It encodes the §6 security flags (which are non-negotiable) and the §6a judging contract (which has subtle traps that produce wrong-answer disputes).
- webide-spec: Use this skill at the start of any implementation session for the WebIde competitive coding platform — when editing any .NET project (WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.DAL, WebIde.Model, WebIde.Worker), Docker config, GitHub workflow, nginx config, or sandbox script. It orients you to the project state, the source-of-truth spec, and the phased implementation contract.
- webide-worker: Use this skill when working in WebIde.Worker/ — creating the project, writing SubmissionWorker, SandboxOrchestrator, SubmissionEvaluator, HeartbeatService, StuckSubmissionReaper, or wiring its Program.cs. It encodes the DI lifetime rules (IDbContextFactory + DockerClient + IConnectionMultiplexer), graceful shutdown for the BackgroundService, and the Docker.DotNet memory-stats and DooD pitfalls from §10 and §14 of docs/deployment-handoff.md.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	You are implementing the deployment of a competitive coding platform called WebIde. The complete specification is at /Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide/docs/deployment-handoff.md — read it first, in full, before doing anything else. It is ~750 lines and is the source of truth. Do not improvise around it.
2	
3	## Repo context
4	
5	- Working directory: /Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide
6	- Solution: WebIde.slnx (projects: WebIde.Model, WebIde.DAL, WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.Console; empty WebIde.Api dir — ignore)
7	- Branch: develop (main is the deploy target)
8	- .NET 10, EF Core 9, PostgreSQL, Razor MVC. No JS build pipeline currently. No tests project.
9	- The current Program.cs has no auth, no Redis, no SignalR. The User entity has no GitHubId/AvatarUrl yet. The data model is otherwise complete.
10	- _Sidebar.cshtml has a hardcoded "ROOT_USER" string and a hardcoded single-letter "R" avatar that need to be replaced.
11	
12	## What I want you to do this session
13	
14	**Implement Phase 0 (Infrastructure) only.** Do not start Phase 1. See §7 of the handoff doc for the file list and the "Done when" criterion. That is roughly:
15	- docker-compose.yml with all 8 services + log rotation + internal-only networking for postgres/redis/seq
16	- nginx/nginx.conf per §10 with TLS, rate-limit zones, /hubs/ WebSocket upgrade, basic-auth on /seq/
17	- Dockerfile.app (multi-stage: node tailwind build → sdk build → aspnet runtime)
18	- Dockerfile.worker
19	- sandbox/{gcc,python,node}.Dockerfile + the three wrapper scripts implementing §6a's judging contract
20	- tailwind/input.css + tailwind.config.js to replace the Tailwind Play CDN
21	- .env.template per §9a
22	- (Worker .csproj does not exist yet — you'll create it in Phase 2, not now. Compose can reference the future image.)
23	
24	Stop after Phase 0 is done. Do not move to Phase 1 in this session.
25	
26	## Non-negotiable rules
27	
28	1. The §6 docker run flag list is exact. Do not modify it. In particular: `--pids-limit 64` (not `--ulimit nproc`), `--user nobody:nogroup`, `--network none`, `--read-only`, `--cap-drop ALL`, image pinned by digest.
29	2. The §6a judging contract is the spec for the wrapper scripts. Implement output normalization, float tolerance, multi-case model, stdout/stderr caps, and per-case verdict precedence exactly as written. If anything in §6a is ambiguous, ASK — do not guess. Wrong-answer bugs here are catastrophic.
30	3. HTTPS is required day 1 (§11b). Self-signed cert for local dev is fine; structure compose and nginx so certbot can take over in prod without code changes.
31	4. No `:latest` tags in production references. Pin sandbox images by digest via env var (§9a). The deploy pipeline (Phase 5, later) promotes `:latest` only after health check passes.
32	5. Postgres, Redis, Seq must NOT publish ports to the host. Only nginx publishes 80/443.
33	
34	## What you should NOT do
35	
36	- Do NOT make any changes to source code in WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.Model, WebIde.DAL. That's Phase 1.
37	- Do NOT create the Worker project. That's Phase 2.
38	- Do NOT attempt: provisioning the Hetzner VPS, creating the GitHub OAuth app, setting branch protection, running certbot's first cert, choosing the backup offsite target, generating production secrets. These are human-only tasks listed below.
39	- Do NOT add code I didn't ask for (no health endpoints, no controllers, no Program.cs edits in this session).
40	- Do NOT use the Tailwind Play CDN. The §7 Phase 0 file list replaces it with a build-time compile.
41	
42	## Human-only tasks (do not attempt; just list them at the end of your work for me)
43	
44	1. Provision the Hetzner VPS and run §11 server setup.
45	2. Create the GitHub OAuth app per §12; copy Client ID + Secret to /opt/webide/.env.
46	3. Set GitHub branch protection on `main` and add HETZNER_HOST, HETZNER_USER, HETZNER_SSH_KEY secrets (§12a).
47	4. Run the initial `certbot certonly --webroot ...` to mint the first cert (§11b).
48	5. Decide the backup offsite target (Hetzner Storage Box / B2 / S3) and provision credentials (§11c).
49	6. Generate POSTGRES_PASSWORD, REDIS_PASSWORD, SEQ_BASIC_AUTH_HTPASSWD per §9a.
50	
51	## How to work
52	
53	- Verify each file against the spec before writing the next one. The doc cross-references heavily; missing details in one section are usually pinned down in another (§10, §6a, §9a especially).
54	- After all Phase 0 files exist, run the Phase 0 acceptance check from §7: `docker compose up` brings the stack up locally, `/health` returns 200, HTTPS works against a self-signed cert, and the fork-bomb test from §13b kills the container without disturbing the host.
55	- If something in the spec is genuinely unclear or seems wrong, stop and ask me. Don't invent.
56	- End your session with: (a) list of files created, (b) the Phase 0 acceptance results, (c) the human-only checklist with status, (d) any open questions for Phase 1.
57

## Repo context

- Working directory: /Users/fbeleta/Documents/Web-ide/Web_ide
- Solution: WebIde.slnx (projects: WebIde.Model, WebIde.DAL, WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.Console; empty WebIde.Api dir — ignore)
- Branch: develop (main is the deploy target)

- _Sidebar.cshtml has a hardcoded "ROOT_USER" string and a hardcoded single-letter "R" avatar that need to be replaced.

## What I want you to do this session

**Implement Phase 0 (Infrastructure) only.** Do not start Phase 1. See §7 of the handoff doc for the file list and the "Done when" criterion. That is roughly:
- docker-compose.yml with all 8 services + log rotation + internal-only networking for postgres/redis/seq
- nginx/nginx.conf per §10 with TLS, rate-limit zones, /hubs/ WebSocket upgrade, basic-auth on /seq/
- Dockerfile.app (multi-stage: node tailwind build → sdk build → aspnet runtime)
- Dockerfile.worker
- sandbox/{gcc,python,node}.Dockerfile + the three wrapper scripts implementing §6a's judging contract
- tailwind/input.css + tailwind.config.js to replace the Tailwind Play CDN
- .env.template per §9a
- (Worker .csproj does not exist yet — you'll create it in Phase 2, not now. Compose can reference the future image.)

Stop after Phase 0 is done. Do not move to Phase 1 in this session.

## Non-negotiable rules

1. The §6 docker run flag list is exact. Do not modify it. In particular: `--pids-limit 64` (not `--ulimit nproc`), `--user nobody:nogroup`, `--network none`, `--read-only`, `--cap-drop ALL`, image pinned by digest.
2. The §6a judging contract is the spec for the wrapper scripts. Implement output normalization, float tolerance, multi-case model, stdout/stderr caps, and per-case verdict precedence exactly as written. If anything in §6a is ambiguous, ASK — do not guess. Wrong-answer bugs here are catastrophic.
3. HTTPS is required day 1 (§11b). Self-signed cert for local dev is fine; structure compose and nginx so certbot can take over in prod without code changes.
4. No `:latest` tags in production references. Pin sandbox images by digest via env var (§9a). The deploy pipeline (Phase 5, later) promotes `:latest` only after health check passes.
5. Postgres, Redis, Seq must NOT publish ports to the host. Only nginx publishes 80/443.

## What you should NOT do

- Do NOT make any changes to source code in WebIde.Frontend, WebIde.Model, WebIde.DAL. That's Phase 1.
- Do NOT create the Worker project. That's Phase 2.
- Do NOT attempt: provisioning the Hetzner VPS, creating the GitHub OAuth app, setting branch protection, running certbot's first cert, choosing the backup offsite target, generating production secrets. These are human-only tasks listed below.
- Do NOT add code I didn't ask for (no health endpoints, no controllers, no Program.cs edits in this session).
- Do NOT use the Tailwind Play CDN. The §7 Phase 0 file list replaces it with a build-time compile.

## Human-only tasks (do not attempt; just list them at the end of your work for me)

1. Provision the Hetzner VPS and run §11 server setup.
2. Create the GitHub OAuth app per §12; copy Client ID + Secret to /opt/webide/.env.
3. Set GitHub branch protection on `main` and add HETZNER_HOST, HETZNER_USER, HETZNER_SSH_KEY secrets (§12a).
4. Run the initial `certbot certonly --webroot ...` to mint the first cert (§11b).
5. Decide the backup offsite target (Hetzner Storage Box / B2 / S3) and provision credentials (§11c).
6. Generate POSTGRES_PASSWORD, REDIS_PASSWORD, SEQ_BASIC_AUTH_HTPASSWD per §9a.

## How to work

- Verify each file against the spec before writing the next one. The doc cross-references heavily; missing details in one section are usually pinned down in another (§10, §6a, §9a especially).
- After all Phase 0 files exist, run the Phase 0 acceptance check from §7: `docker compose up` brings the stack up locally, `/health` returns 200, HTTPS works against a self-signed cert, and the fork-bomb test from §13b kills the container without disturbing the host.
- If something in the spec is genuinely unclear or seems wrong, stop and ask me. Don't invent.
- End your session with: (a) list of files created, (b) the Phase 0 acceptance results, (c) the human-only checklist with status, (d) any open questions for Phase 1.

content.content:
1	# WebIde — Deployment Handoff Document
2	
3	**Date:** 2026-06-06 (rev. 2026-06-06 — production-grade pass)
4	**Author:** Claude Code
5	**Status:** Plan approved — ready for implementation
6	**Branch:** develop → main
7	
8	---
9	
10	## 1. What This Document Is
11	
12	A complete handoff specification for taking WebIde from a read-only local .NET app to a production-ready, auto-deploying competitive coding platform on Hetzner. Any engineer picking this up should be able to implement end-to-end from this document alone.
13	
14	This revision treats the platform as **production-grade**: real users, hostile submissions, HTTPS required, persistent data, recoverable from a single-host failure.
15	
16	---
17	
18	## 2. Current State
19	
20	| Area | Status |
21	|---|---|
22	| ASP.NET MVC web app | ✅ Working (read-only, no auth) |
23	| PostgreSQL + EF Core | ✅ Fully integrated, migrations exist |
24	| Tailwind CSS + Razor views | ✅ Brutalist design system applied (via Tailwind Play CDN — must be replaced, see §7 Phase 0) |
25	| Code execution | ❌ Data model exists, no implementation |
26	| Authentication | ❌ None |
27	| Docker / docker-compose | ❌ Not created |
28	| CI/CD | ❌ Not created |
29	| Nginx + HTTPS | ❌ Not created |
30	
31	**Scope note for v1:** `ProblemSet`, `Organization`, and `Tag` entities exist in the model but ship as **read-only seeded data**. No admin UI in v1; managed via SQL until the admin-UI phase (§15).
32	
33	---
34	
35	## 3. Target Architecture
36	
37	```
38	                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
39	                    │              Hetzner VPS                     │
40	                    │         (4 cores / 8 GB RAM)                 │
41	                    │                                              │
42	Internet ──HTTPS──▶ │  nginx :80/:443  (TLS termination, HSTS)     │
43	                    │      │                                       │
44	                    │      ├──/auth/* /submissions/* (rate limited)│
45	                    │      ├──/hubs/* (WebSocket upgrade)          │
46	                    │      └──/seq/* (basic-auth gated)            │
47	                    │      │                                       │
48	                    │      ▼                                       │
49	                    │  webide-app :8080  (ASP.NET MVC + SignalR)   │
50	                    │      │  ▲                                    │
51	                    │      │  │ pub/sub (SignalR backplane)        │
52	                    │   queue│ │                                   │
53	                    │      ▼  │                                    │
54	                    │  webide-worker (.NET Worker Service)         │
55	                    │      │                                       │
56	                    │      ▼ Docker socket (DooD)                  │
57	                    │  sandbox containers (sibling, ephemeral)     │
58	                    │      gcc / python / node                     │
59	                    │                                              │
60	                    │  postgres  redis  seq   (Docker net only)    │
61	                    │  (no published ports)                        │
62	                    └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
63	```
64	
65	### Why Docker-out-of-Docker (DooD)?
66	The worker mounts `/var/run/docker.sock` from the host so sandbox containers run as siblings (not children) on the host daemon. This avoids the `--privileged` requirement of Docker-in-Docker and is simpler to operate on a single-node deployment. The worker container itself never runs user code; sandbox containers have zero permissions (§6).
67	
68	### Network exposure
69	- Only nginx publishes ports (80, 443) to the host.
70	- `postgres`, `redis`, `seq` listen on the internal `webide-net` Docker network only — **no `ports:` directive** in compose. Inspect Postgres with `docker compose exec postgres psql`; access Seq via `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/seq/` behind nginx basic auth.
71	
72	---
73	
74	## 4. Resource Allocation
75	
76	| Service | CPU limit | RAM limit | Notes |
77	|---|---|---|---|
78	| nginx | 0.1 | 64 MB | Reverse proxy + TLS |
79	| webide-app | 0.5 | 512 MB | ASP.NET MVC + SignalR |
80	| webide-worker | 0.3 | 256 MB | Manages containers, doesn't run code |
81	| postgres | 0.5 | 1 GB | Primary datastore (tune `shared_buffers=256MB`) |
82	| redis | 0.1 | 128 MB | Queue + sessions + SignalR backplane + DataProtection keys |
83	| seq | 0.3 | 512 MB | Log aggregation (14-day retention) |
84	| postgres-backup | 0.1 | 64 MB | Nightly `pg_dump`, rsync to offsite |
85	| **sandbox slot 1** | **0.9** | **512 MB** | Per-run limit, enforced by Docker |
86	| **sandbox slot 2** | **0.9** | **512 MB** | Max 2 concurrent at once |
87	| OS overhead | 0.3 | 512 MB | — |
88	| **Total** | **4.0** | **~4 GB** | Leaves ~4 GB headroom |
89	
90	Sandbox CPU intentionally set to 0.9 (not 1.0) so the app/worker keep responsiveness under burst load — CFS throttling on the user-facing path is worse than slightly slower judging.
91	
92	**Dynamic slot calculation** (for future server upgrades):
93	```
94	slots = min(
95	  floor((totalRam     - reservedRam) / sandboxMemMb),
96	  floor((totalCpu     - reservedCpu) / sandboxCpu)
97	)
98	```
99	Both bounds matter. RAM-only is wrong on small-core boxes; CPU-only is wrong on small-RAM boxes. Controlled by `Worker__MaxConcurrentSandboxes` env var (override if you want fewer slots than the formula allows).
100	
101	---
102	
103	## 5. End-to-End Submission Flow
104	
105	```
106	1. User → GET /problems/{id}
107	         └─ Monaco editor (CDN), language picker, Submit button
108	
109	2. User clicks Submit
110	         └─ fetch POST /submissions (JSON: problemId, language, sourceCode)
111	            with header RequestVerificationToken: <antiforgery>
112	         └─ Rate limiter: 5/min per user (429 on excess)
113	         └─ Antiforgery validated via IAntiforgery.ValidateRequestAsync
114	         └─ Submission record created (status = Pending)
115	         └─ SubmissionJob JSON pushed to Redis list "submissions:queue"
116	         └─ Response: { submissionId: 42 }
117	
118	3. Browser opens SignalR connection
119	         └─ hub.invoke("SubscribeToSubmission", 42)
120	         └─ Status chip shows: PENDING (spinner)
121	
122	4. Worker (BLPOP)
123	         └─ Deserializes SubmissionJob
124	         └─ Acquires SemaphoreSlim slot (blocks if both slots busy)
125	         └─ Updates Submission.Status → Running
126	         └─ Publishes Running event → browser shows: RUNNING
127	
128	5. Worker spawns ONE sandbox container per submission
129	         └─ Writes source to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/solution.{ext}
130	         └─ Writes test cases to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/cases.json
131	         └─ docker run [see §6 for full flags]
132	         └─ Wrapper iterates cases internally (see §6a)
133	         └─ Wrapper exits with summary; per-case results in /tmp/result.json
134	         └─ Worker reads results from stdout (capped) + container exit
135	
136	6. Container finishes (or is killed)
137	         └─ Worker collects per-case verdicts, wall time, peak memory
138	         └─ Evaluator computes final status + score (see §6a)
139	         └─ Writes ExecutionResult + updates Submission.Status
140	         └─ Publishes result to Redis channel "execution:42"
141	
142	7. RedisSubscriptionService (IHostedService in app)
143	         └─ Receives from "execution:*" pattern subscription
144	         └─ Pushes to IHubContext<ExecutionHub>.Clients.Group("submission:42")
145	
146	8. Browser receives ExecutionResult via SignalR
147	         └─ Status chip: ACCEPTED / WRONG_ANSWER / TIME_LIMIT / COMPILE_ERROR / RUNTIME_ERROR
148	         └─ Wall time + memory + per-case verdicts displayed
149	```
150	
151	**Exit codes from sandbox wrapper (final result):**
152	
153	| Exit | Meaning | Maps to status |
154	|---|---|---|
155	| 0 | All cases handled (per-case verdicts in JSON) | Computed from JSON: Accepted/WrongAnswer/TLE/MLE/RuntimeError |
156	| 2 | Compile failed (C/C++ only) | CompileError |
157	| 124 | Wrapper itself timed out (defense-in-depth) | TimeLimitExceeded |
158	| ≠ 0,2,124 | Wrapper crash | InternalError |
159	
160	Note: Python and Node have no compile step. A `SyntaxError` at runtime exits 1 and is mapped to `RuntimeError`, **not** `CompileError`. This is intentional: there is no separate compile phase to fail in, so a parse error is a runtime error from the platform's perspective. UI should still surface the stderr so users see the syntax error.
161	
162	---
163	
164	## 6. Sandbox Security Model
165	
166	Every sandbox container runs with these exact Docker flags — **non-negotiable**:
167	
168	```bash
169	docker run --rm \
170	  --network none \              # zero network access
171	  --read-only \                 # root filesystem immutable
172	  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m,mode=1777 \
173	  --memory $MEMORY_MB \         # min(problem.MemoryLimitKb/1024, 512)
174	  --memory-swap $MEMORY_MB \    # disable swap (no swap escape)
175	  --cpus 0.9 \                  # matches §4 slot allocation
176	  --pids-limit 64 \             # cgroup PIDs cap — defeats fork bombs
177	  --user nobody:nogroup \       # non-root inside container
178	  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
179	  --cap-drop ALL \              # drop all Linux capabilities
180	  --ulimit fsize=67108864 \     # 64 MB max file size (defense in depth)
181	  -v /tmp/webide-src/{id}:/code:ro \  # source + cases: read-only
182	  webide-sandbox-{lang}@sha256:{digest} \
183	  /code/solution.{ext} /code/cases.json
184	```
185	
186	### Why `--pids-limit`, not `--ulimit nproc`
187	`--ulimit nproc` is a *per-UID rlimit*, applied at the host level. With containers sharing UIDs, nproc aggregates across containers and the host, so a fork bomb can exhaust the host's UID budget even when contained. `--pids-limit` is a *cgroup pids controller* — strictly per-container — and is the correct primitive.
188	
189	### Why `--user nobody:nogroup`
190	Defense in depth. Even with `--cap-drop ALL` and `no-new-privileges`, root inside a namespace has more attack surface (mount, ptrace, specific syscalls). `nobody:nogroup` costs nothing and reduces it. Each sandbox Dockerfile must add `RUN adduser -D -H -u 65534 nobody 2>/dev/null || true && addgroup -S nogroup 2>/dev/null || true` (Alpine).
191	
192	### Why pin image digests
193	`@sha256:{digest}` rather than `:latest` makes the sandbox behavior reproducible across deploys. Without it, a base-image refresh changes language minor versions silently and breaks judging determinism.
194	
195	**What code CANNOT do:**
196	- Make network requests (outbound or inbound) — `--network none`
197	- Write to filesystem (except `/tmp`, capped 64 MB) — `--read-only`
198	- Fork more than 64 processes — `--pids-limit`
199	- Escalate privileges — `--cap-drop ALL` + `no-new-privileges`
200	- Read other submissions' source files — isolated host dir + read-only mount
201	- Communicate with any other container — no network
202	- Swap to disk — `--memory-swap` matches `--memory`
203	
204	**C/C++ compile strategy:** A `compile-and-run.sh` wrapper inside the gcc image compiles to `/tmp/a.out` then runs it against each test case. Exit 2 = compile failure. For Python/Node, the wrapper directly invokes the interpreter per case.
205	
206	---
207	
208	## 6a. Judging Contract
209	
210	Without this section, "WRONG_ANSWER" disputes will dominate the bug tracker. These rules are normative.
211	
212	### Multi-test-case execution model
213	- One sandbox container per submission.
214	- Wrapper script reads `/code/cases.json` (array of `{ id, stdin, expected }`).
215	- For each case: spawn the user program as a subprocess, pipe `stdin`, capture stdout/stderr, time it, kill at `problem.TimeLimitMs + 500ms` grace.
216	- Wrapper writes `/tmp/result.json` with `[{ id, verdict, wallMs, peakKb, stdout, stderr }, …]`.
217	- Wrapper prints `result.json` to stdout (capped, see below) for the worker to ingest.
218	
219	### Output normalization (applied before comparison)
220	1. Convert CRLF → LF.
221	2. Strip trailing whitespace on each line.
222	3. Strip trailing blank lines.
223	4. Internal whitespace preserved (do not collapse).
224	
225	### Float comparison
226	- Per-problem flag `Problem.FloatTolerance` (double, nullable). If null → exact string match after normalization.
227	- If non-null → tokenize both expected and actual on whitespace; lengths must match; each pair compared via `|a - b| ≤ tol ∨ |a - b| ≤ tol × |b|` (combined absolute + relative).
228	- Schema change: add `double? FloatTolerance` to `Problem`. Default null. Migration: `AddFloatToleranceToProblem`.
229	
230	### Per-case verdict precedence
231	For a single case: `MemoryLimitExceeded` > `TimeLimitExceeded` > `RuntimeError` > `WrongAnswer` > `Accepted`.
232	
233	### Submission-level scoring
234	- `Submission.Score = Σ TestCase.Points` over accepted cases.
235	- `Submission.Status` is the worst per-case verdict by the precedence above. (`Accepted` only if every case is Accepted.)
236	
237	### Stdout/stderr caps
238	- Per-case: 4 MB stdout, 1 MB stderr. Truncate with marker `\n[truncated: N bytes elided]\n`.
239	- Wrapper-level output to worker: 16 MB JSON max. Worker truncates `stdout`/`stderr` fields in DB to 64 KB.
240	
241	### Wall time and memory measurement
242	- Wall time: measured by the wrapper around each user-process invocation (clock_gettime CLOCK_MONOTONIC), **not** Docker daemon timestamps for the whole container — that includes interpreter warm-up and is unfair.
243	- Peak memory: `getrusage(RUSAGE_CHILDREN).ru_maxrss` after each subprocess exit. On Linux this is in KB and is per-process; for multi-process programs use cgroup `memory.peak` from inside the container if available.
244

330	
331	## 8. GitHub Actions Deployment Pipeline
332	
333	### CI Gate (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
334	Triggers: push to `develop`, PR to `main`
335	
336	```
337	checkout → setup-dotnet 10.x → dotnet restore → dotnet build → dotnet test
338	```
339	
340	Branch protection rule on `main`: this workflow must pass before merge (see §12a).
341	
342	### Sandbox Pipeline (`.github/workflows/sandbox.yml`)
343	Triggers: push to `main` where `sandbox/**` changed.
344	
345	```
346	1. Login to ghcr.io
347	2. For each lang in [gcc, python, node]:
348	   - docker buildx build sandbox/{lang}.Dockerfile
349	   - Push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/sandbox-{lang}:sha-{SHA}
350	3. Output digests as workflow artifact for deploy.yml consumption
351	```
352	
353	Sandbox images are pinned by digest in deploy. They are **not** rebuilt on every deploy — only when their source changes.
354	
355	### Deploy Pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`)
356	Triggers: push to `main`
357	
358	```
359	1. Login to ghcr.io
360	2. Capture rollback target:
361	     PREV_DIGEST=$(docker buildx imagetools inspect ghcr.io/.../app:latest --format '{{json .Manifest.Digest}}')
362	     # Saved for rollback even if :latest gets overwritten
363	3. Build + push app:sha-{SHA}, worker:sha-{SHA}
364	     (Do NOT push :latest yet.)
365	4. SSH into Hetzner (appleboy/ssh-action):
366	   a. cd /opt/webide
367	   b. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose pull webide-app webide-worker
368	   c. Run migrations (see §10a):
369	        docker run --rm --env-file .env --network webide_webide-net \
370	          ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} \
371	          dotnet ef database update --no-build
372	   d. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose up -d webide-app webide-worker
373	   e. Poll GET https://localhost/health (30 attempts × 2s = 60s timeout)
374	        Also poll /health/ready which checks worker heartbeat.
375	   f. ✅ Pass:
376	        - From CI: re-tag and push ghcr.io/.../app:sha-{SHA} as :latest
377	        - docker image prune -f
378	   g. ❌ Fail:
379	        IMAGE_TAG=${PREV_DIGEST} docker compose up -d
380	        (Rolls back to the digest captured in step 2 — not :latest, which may have been overwritten.)
381	```
382	
383	**Required GitHub Secrets:**
384	
385	| Secret | Value |
386	|---|---|
387	| `HETZNER_HOST` | Server IP address |
388	| `HETZNER_USER` | SSH username (e.g. `deploy`) |
389	| `HETZNER_SSH_KEY` | Private SSH key (Ed25519 preferred) |
390	
391	**Blue-green mechanism:** `IMAGE_TAG` env var controls which image Docker Compose uses. The `:latest` tag is promoted **only after** the health check confirms the new container is healthy. On failure, rollback uses the digest captured at step 2 (saved as `PREV_DIGEST`), guaranteeing we restore the previous known-good image even if step 3 had partially overwritten registry tags.
392	
393	**Important:** The migration `docker run` must use `--network webide_webide-net` so it can reach the `postgres` service by hostname. Migrations follow the discipline in §10a — they must succeed against both the previous-version app and the new-version app to allow rollback after migration.

459	## 10. Key Implementation Notes
460	
461	### `Program.cs` middleware order (critical)
462	```csharp
463	app.UseForwardedHeaders(new ForwardedHeadersOptions {
464	    ForwardedHeaders = ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedFor | ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedProto,
465	    KnownNetworks = { /* nginx subnet */ }, KnownProxies = { /* nginx IP */ }
466	});
467	app.UseHsts();              // Production only
468	app.UseHttpsRedirection();
469	app.UseRouting();
470	app.UseRateLimiter();
471	app.UseAuthentication();    // MUST be before Authorization
472	app.UseAuthorization();
473	app.UseSession();
474	app.MapHub<ExecutionHub>("/hubs/execution").RequireRateLimiting("hub");
475	app.MapHealthChecks("/health");
476	app.MapHealthChecks("/health/ready", new HealthCheckOptions { Predicate = c => c.Tags.Contains("ready") });
477	app.MapControllerRoute(/* ... */);
478	```
479	
480	### DataProtection on Redis (critical)
481	Without this, every container restart logs every user out and invalidates every antiforgery token (keys are regenerated in the container's ephemeral filesystem).
482	
483	```csharp
484	var redis = ConnectionMultiplexer.Connect(redisConnectionString);
485	services.AddSingleton<IConnectionMultiplexer>(redis);
486	services.AddDataProtection()
487	    .PersistKeysToStackExchangeRedis(redis, "DataProtection-Keys")
488	    .SetApplicationName("WebIde");
489	```
490	
491	### Cookie policy (critical)
492	```csharp
493	services.ConfigureApplicationCookie(o => {
494	    o.Cookie.HttpOnly = true;
495	    o.Cookie.SecurePolicy = CookieSecurePolicy.Always;
496	    o.Cookie.SameSite = SameSiteMode.Lax;   // Lax: OAuth callback needs it
497	    o.ExpireTimeSpan = TimeSpan.FromDays(14);
498	    o.SlidingExpiration = true;
499	    o.LoginPath = "/auth/github/login";
500	});
501	```
502	
503	### Rate limiter
504	```csharp
505	services.AddRateLimiter(o => {
506	    o.AddFixedWindowLimiter("submission", opt => {
507	        opt.PermitLimit = 5; opt.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
508	    });
509	    o.AddTokenBucketLimiter("auth", opt => {
510	        opt.TokenLimit = 10; opt.TokensPerPeriod = 10;
511	        opt.ReplenishmentPeriod = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
512	    });
513	    o.RejectionStatusCode = 429;
514	});
515	```
516	Apply via `[EnableRateLimiting("submission")]` on `SubmissionController.Submit`, `[EnableRateLimiting("auth")]` on `AuthController`.
517	
518	### Antiforgery for JSON POST (important)
519	Default `[ValidateAntiForgeryToken]` only validates the form-encoded token. For fetch + JSON, do:
520	```csharp
521	[HttpPost]
522	[Authorize]
523	[EnableRateLimiting("submission")]
524	public async Task<IActionResult> Submit([FromBody] SubmitDto dto, [FromServices] IAntiforgery antiforgery) {
525	    await antiforgery.ValidateRequestAsync(HttpContext);  // reads token from header
526	    /* ... */
527	}
528	```
529	Razor view emits the token:
530	```html
531	@Html.AntiForgeryToken()
532	<script>
533	  const token = document.querySelector('input[name="__RequestVerificationToken"]').value;
534	  await fetch('/submissions', {
535	    method: 'POST',
536	    headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 'RequestVerificationToken': token },
537	    body: JSON.stringify({ problemId, language, sourceCode })
538	  });
539	</script>
540	```
541	
542	### DI lifetime cheat sheet (critical)
543	| Service | Lifetime | Reason |
544	|---|---|---|
545	| `IConnectionMultiplexer` | Singleton | StackExchange.Redis is designed as a single shared connection |
546	| `DockerClient` | Singleton | Docker.DotNet thread-safe; expensive to recreate |
547	| `WebIdeDbContext` | Scoped (via `IDbContextFactory`) | Hosted services are singletons; injecting scoped directly throws at startup |
548	| `SandboxOrchestrator` | Singleton | Holds `SemaphoreSlim` slot state |
549	| `SubmissionEvaluator` | Scoped | Stateless; scoped is conventional |
550	| `HeartbeatService`, `StuckSubmissionReaper`, `SubmissionWorker`, `RedisSubscriptionService` | Singleton (hosted) | They are `IHostedService` |
551	
552	### Memory stats collection (critical)
553	Set `AutoRemove = false`. Stream stats via `GetContainerStatsAsync` until the container exits; take the max `MemoryStats.Usage` (or `MaxUsage` if exposed) observed. On cgroupv2, prefer reading `memory.peak` from inside the container via the wrapper (more accurate than Docker's polled stats). After the container exits, call `RemoveContainerAsync`. The "inspect before remove" approach in the prior revision was wrong — `InspectContainerAsync` does not return memory stats.
554	
555	### Wall-time measurement (critical)
556	Measured by the wrapper script per user-process invocation (`clock_gettime`), **not** wall-clock around `docker run` (which includes 100-500ms of container creation). See §6a.
557	
558	### Redis SignalR backplane (load-bearing, not optional)
559	This is **not** "in case you scale later." The worker is a separate process. The bridge from Redis pub/sub to SignalR groups is the only path from worker → browser. Register with `.AddSignalR().AddStackExchangeRedis(connectionString)`.
560	
561	### Monaco Editor (CDN)
562	The project has no JS build pipeline. Use AMD loader from CDN:
563	```html
564	<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/monaco-editor@0.47.0/min/vs/loader.js"></script>
565	```
566	Language picker drives Monaco's model language:
567	```js
568	const langMap = { cpp: 'cpp', python: 'python', javascript: 'javascript' };
569	monaco.editor.setModelLanguage(editor.getModel(), langMap[selectedLang]);
570	```
571	On submit, sync Monaco value to a hidden `<textarea name="sourceCode">` and include the language. Antiforgery token in header per above.
572	
573	### Tailwind (not via CDN)
574	Tailwind Play CDN is documented by Tailwind as **not for production**. The Dockerfile.app first stage runs `npx tailwindcss -i tailwind/input.css -o WebIde.Frontend/wwwroot/css/site.tailwind.css --minify`. The `<script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com">` tag in `_Layout.cshtml` is replaced by `<link rel="stylesheet" href="~/css/site.tailwind.css" asp-append-version="true">`.
575	
576	### Health checks
577	- `/health` (liveness): always 200 if the process is up. No DB check (a flapping DB shouldn't take down the load balancer).
578	- `/health/ready` (readiness): checks Postgres connectivity, Redis connectivity, worker heartbeat (must be < 30s old). Returns 503 if any fail. Used by deploy pipeline.
579	
580	### nginx full config sketch
581	```nginx
582	limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=auth:10m rate=30r/m;
583	limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=submit:10m rate=10r/m;
584	server {
585	    listen 443 ssl http2;
586	    server_name webide.example.com;
587	    ssl_certificate     /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../fullchain.pem;
588	    ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/.../privkey.pem;
589	    add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains" always;
590	    gzip on; gzip_types text/css application/javascript application/json;
591	    client_max_body_size 1m;
592	
593	    location /auth/      { limit_req zone=auth burst=10; proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080; include /etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf; }
594	    location /submissions { limit_req zone=submit burst=5; proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080; include /etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf; }
595	    location /hubs/ {
596	        proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080;
597	        proxy_http_version 1.1;
598	        proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
599	        proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
600	        proxy_read_timeout 86400;
601	        include /etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf;
602	    }
603	    location /seq/ { auth_basic "Seq"; auth_basic_user_file /etc/nginx/htpasswd; proxy_pass http://seq:80; include /etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf; }
604	    location /    { proxy_pass http://webide-app:8080; include /etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf; }
605	}
606	server { listen 80; server_name webide.example.com; return 301 https://$host$request_uri; }
607	```
608	`/etc/nginx/proxy_pass.conf`:
609	```nginx
610	proxy_set_header Host $host;
611	proxy_set_header X-Real-IP $remote_addr;
612	proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
613	proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
614	```
615	
616	---
617	
618	## 10a. Migration Discipline
619	
620	Migrations run **before** the new app container starts (§8 step c). This creates an asymmetric window: if migration succeeds but the app health check fails, rollback brings back code that doesn't know the new schema. To make this safe:
621	
622	### Rules for every migration
623	1. **Additive only in a single deploy.** Add nullable columns; never rename or drop a column in the same deploy as the code that uses it. Use the two-deploy pattern:
624	   - Deploy A: add new column (nullable), have code write to both old and new.
625	   - Deploy B (later): switch reads to new column, stop writing to old.
626	   - Deploy C: drop old column.
627	2. **No `[Required]` on new properties** without a server default — backfill in a data migration first.
628	3. **Forward-only.** No down migrations. Rollback for a failed deploy that already migrated = restore the most recent Postgres backup (§11c) and redeploy the previous image. This is documented in the runbook, not automated, because each case needs human judgment.
629	
630	### What this trades off
631	Compared to fully online schema changes (concurrent index builds, blue-green schema, etc.) we accept a possible brief outage if a deploy ships an incompatible migration. This is acceptable given the user scale and the explicit pattern above.
632	
633	---
634	
635	## 11. Server Setup (One-Time, Manual)
636	
637	Run on the Hetzner server before first deploy:
638	
639	```bash
640	# Install Docker
641	curl -fsSL https://get.docker.com | sh
642	
643	# Create deploy user
644	useradd -m -s /bin/bash deploy
645	usermod -aG docker deploy
646	
647	# Create app directory
648	mkdir -p /opt/webide
649	chown deploy:deploy /opt/webide
650	
651	# Create source temp dir (worker bind mount) — mode 700, owned by deploy
652	mkdir -p /tmp/webide-src
653	chown deploy:deploy /tmp/webide-src
654	chmod 700 /tmp/webide-src
655	# Worker container must run as the host's deploy UID to read/write this dir.
656	# Set `user: "${DEPLOY_UID}:${DEPLOY_GID}"` in compose for webide-worker.
657	
658	# Copy .env from template (fill in secrets — see §9a)
659	cp .env.template /opt/webide/.env
660	chmod 600 /opt/webide/.env
661	
662	# Generate Seq basic-auth credentials for nginx
663	htpasswd -B -c /opt/webide/nginx/htpasswd ops    # prompts for password
664	
665	# Add SSH public key for deploy user (GitHub Actions will use this)
666	mkdir -p /home/deploy/.ssh
667	echo "YOUR_PUBLIC_KEY" >> /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
668	chmod 700 /home/deploy/.ssh
669	chmod 600 /home/deploy/.ssh/authorized_keys
670	```
671	
672	Sandbox images are NOT pulled manually here. They are built by `.github/workflows/sandbox.yml` and pinned by digest via the `SANDBOX_*_DIGEST` env vars (§9a). The worker pulls them on first job if missing.
673	
674	---
675	
676	## 11b. HTTPS / TLS (Day 1)
677	
678	HTTPS is **required from day 1**. GitHub OAuth's correlation cookie defaults to `Secure=true` after the redirect; cookie auth requires `Secure=Always` in production. Without HTTPS, auth literally does not function.
679	
680	### Approach: Certbot in a sidecar container
681	```yaml
682	# excerpt of docker-compose.yml
683	certbot:
684	  image: certbot/certbot
685	  volumes:
686	    - certbot-etc:/etc/letsencrypt
687	    - certbot-var:/var/lib/letsencrypt
688	    - ./nginx/acme-challenge:/var/www/acme:rw
689	  entrypoint: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
690	  command: |
691	    "trap exit TERM; while :; do
692	       certbot renew --webroot -w /var/www/acme --quiet;
693	       sleep 12h & wait $${!};
694	     done;"
695	
696	nginx:
697	  # mount certbot-etc:/etc/letsencrypt:ro
698	  # serve /var/www/acme via location /.well-known/acme-challenge/
699	```
700	
701	### Initial bootstrap (one-time, on server)
702	```bash
703	docker compose run --rm certbot certonly --webroot \
704	  -w /var/www/acme \
705	  -d webide.example.com \
706	  -m "$LETSENCRYPT_EMAIL" --agree-tos --non-interactive
707	docker compose restart nginx
708	```
709	
710	### App-side requirements
711	- `app.UseForwardedHeaders(...)` **before** any auth middleware so `Request.Scheme = https`.
712	- `app.UseHsts()` in production.
713	- Cookie policy with `SecurePolicy = CookieSecurePolicy.Always` (§10).
714	- OAuth callback registered as `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/auth/github/callback`.
715	
716	---
717	
718	## 11c. Postgres Backup
719	
720	Single-VPS production without backup = guaranteed data loss event. Required day 1.
721	
722	### In-compose backup service
723	Use `prodrigestivill/postgres-backup-local` (well-maintained, configurable cron, retention):
724	```yaml
725	postgres-backup:
726	  image: prodrigestivill/postgres-backup-local
727	  environment:
728	    POSTGRES_HOST: postgres
729	    POSTGRES_DB: ${POSTGRES_DB}
730	    POSTGRES_USER: ${POSTGRES_USER}
731	    POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD}
732	    SCHEDULE: "@daily"
733	    BACKUP_KEEP_DAYS: 7
734	    BACKUP_KEEP_WEEKS: 4
735	    BACKUP_KEEP_MONTHS: 6
736	  volumes:
737	    - postgres-backups:/backups
738	  depends_on: [postgres]
739	```
740	
741	### Offsite (REQUIRED — local backup is not a backup)
742	Add a small sidecar that rsyncs `postgres-backups/` to a Hetzner Storage Box or S3-compatible bucket nightly:
743	```yaml
744	backup-offsite:
745	  image: instrumentisto/rsync-ssh
746	  volumes:
747	    - postgres-backups:/backups:ro
748	    - ./backup-ssh-key:/root/.ssh/id_ed25519:ro
749	  environment:
750	    SCHEDULE: "0 3 * * *"
751	    REMOTE: "${BACKUP_OFFSITE_TARGET}"
752	  # entrypoint runs rsync on cron schedule
753	```
754	(Or wire a Restic container against a B2/S3 endpoint.)
755	
756	### Restore drill
757	A backup that has never been restored is not a backup. **Quarterly**: pull the latest offsite dump to a scratch VPS, restore into a fresh Postgres, smoke-test. Capture the procedure in a runbook.
758	
759	---
760	
761	## 12. GitHub OAuth App Setup
762	
763	1. Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → OAuth Apps → New OAuth App
764	2. **Application name:** WebIde
765	3. **Homepage URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN`
766	4. **Authorization callback URL:** `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/auth/github/callback`
767	5. Copy Client ID and Client Secret → add to `/opt/webide/.env` (`GITHUB_CLIENT_ID`, `GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET`)
768	
769	---
770	
771	## 12a. GitHub Repo Setup (One-Time)
772	
773	Easy to forget; without these, CI is theatre.
774	
775	- Branch protection on `main`:
776	  - Require status check: `ci.yml / build-and-test`
777	  - Require pull request review before merge
778	  - Disallow force push
779	  - Disallow deletion
780	- Actions permissions:
781	  - Workflows: read & write
782	  - GITHUB_TOKEN can push to ghcr.io (default `write` for own packages)
783	- Secrets: `HETZNER_HOST`, `HETZNER_USER`, `HETZNER_SSH_KEY` (see §8)
784	- Variables (optional, non-secret): `GHCR_NAMESPACE = fbeleta/web_ide`
785	
786	---
787	
788	## 13. Verification Checklist
789	
790	### Infrastructure smoke test
791	- [ ] `docker compose up -d` brings the full stack up
792	- [ ] `curl https://localhost/health` returns 200 (with `-k` against a local self-signed cert)
793	- [ ] `curl https://localhost/health/ready` returns 200 (DB + Redis + worker heartbeat all green)
794	- [ ] Seq UI at `https://localhost/seq/` prompts for basic auth, then renders
795	- [ ] Stopping postgres → `/health/ready` 503; `/health` still 200
796	- [ ] Stopping worker → `/health/ready` 503 within 30s
797	
798	### Worker integration test
799	```bash
800	redis-cli -a "$REDIS_PASSWORD" RPUSH submissions:queue \
801	  '{"SubmissionId":1,"ProblemId":1,"Language":"python","SourceCode":"print(input())","TimeLimitMs":2000,"MemoryLimitKb":524288}'
802	# Check Seq for worker logs
803	# Check DB: SELECT status, wall_time_ms FROM submissions WHERE id = 1;
804	```
805	
806	### Full flow test
807	- [ ] Login via GitHub OAuth → user record created in DB with `GitHubId`
808	- [ ] Submit correct Python solution → ACCEPTED, wall time displayed, per-case verdicts shown
809	- [ ] Submit infinite loop → TIME_LIMIT_EXCEEDED after ~`TimeLimitMs + 500ms`
810	- [ ] Submit wrong output → WRONG_ANSWER
811	- [ ] Submit C++ with syntax error → COMPILE_ERROR with stderr shown
812	- [ ] Submit Python with SyntaxError → RUNTIME_ERROR (intentional per §5)
813	- [ ] Submit while 2 jobs are running → third queues, executes when slot frees
814	- [ ] Submit > 5 times in 1 minute → 429 from rate limiter
815	
816	### CI/CD test
817	- [ ] Open PR to main with broken build → CI fails, merge blocked
818	- [ ] Push to main → images built, pushed to ghcr.io with `:sha-{SHA}`, deployed
819	- [ ] After successful deploy → `:latest` updated; before, it points to previous good
820	- [ ] Simulate unhealthy app (set health check to fail) → rollback to previous digest triggers
821	
822	---
823	
824	## 13a. Local Dev Quick-Start
825	
826	For a fresh clone:
827	
828	```bash
829	cp .env.template .env                # local-safe defaults; no real secrets needed
830	docker compose up -d postgres redis seq
831	cd WebIde.Frontend
832	dotnet ef database update --project ../WebIde.DAL
833	dotnet run                            # http://localhost:5xxx
834	```
835	
836	Worker locally:
837	```bash
838	cd WebIde.Worker
839	dotnet run
840	```
841	
842	Sandbox images locally:
843	```bash
844	docker build -t webide-sandbox-python -f sandbox/python.Dockerfile sandbox/
845	docker build -t webide-sandbox-gcc    -f sandbox/gcc.Dockerfile    sandbox/
846	docker build -t webide-sandbox-node   -f sandbox/node.Dockerfile   sandbox/
847	```
848	Set `SANDBOX_*_DIGEST` to local tags (`webide-sandbox-python` etc.) for local dev.
849	
850	OAuth in local dev: the GitHub OAuth app needs a separate "WebIde-Dev" registration with callback `http://localhost:5xxx/auth/github/callback` and a relaxed cookie policy (Development env auto-disables `SecurePolicy.Always`).
851	
852	---
853	
854	## 13b. Adversarial Verification
855	
856	Run these against the deployed stack on a regular cadence (after any sandbox image change, before each release).
857	
858	```bash
859	# Fork bomb — should die without disturbing the host
860	echo 'import os
861	while True: os.fork()' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
862	docker run [...same flags as §6...] -v /tmp/test-src:/code:ro webide-sandbox-python ...
863	# Expect: container killed quickly; host `ps` count unaffected.
864	
865	# Memory bomb
866	echo 'a = bytearray(2*1024*1024*1024)' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
867	# Expect: MemoryLimitExceeded, container killed.
868	
869	# Filesystem escape attempt
870	echo 'open("/etc/passwd", "w").write("hi")' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
871	# Expect: PermissionError (read-only fs) — NOT a write.
872	
873	# Network egress
874	echo 'import urllib.request; print(urllib.request.urlopen("https://example.com").read())' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
875	# Expect: connection error (--network none).
876	
877	# Identity check
878	echo 'import os; print(os.getuid())' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
879	# Expect: 65534 (nobody), NOT 0.
880	
881	# Long stdout
882	echo 'print("x" * 100_000_000)' > /tmp/test-src/solution.py
883	# Expect: truncated to 4 MB with marker, NOT host OOM.
884	
885	# Worker SIGTERM mid-job
886	# Start a long submission, then `docker compose restart webide-worker`
887	# Expect: submission either completes or transitions to InternalError; not stuck Running forever.
888	
889	# OAuth state missing
890	# Hit /auth/github/callback?code=foo&state=bar with no correlation cookie
891	# Expect: 400 with user-friendly message, NOT 500.
892	```
893	
894	---
895	
896	## 14. Risks & Mitigations
897	
898	| Risk | Severity | Mitigation |
899	|---|---|---|
900	| Sandbox container escape via Docker socket | Medium | Worker uses hard-coded image names by language enum; sandbox containers have `--network none` + `--cap-drop ALL` + `--user nobody` — cannot reach the socket. Worker code is the trust boundary; treat changes to it as security-sensitive. |
901	| Fork bomb / process exhaustion | Low | `--pids-limit 64` (not `--ulimit nproc`) — cgroup-enforced, per-container. |
902	| `/tmp/webide-src` stale dirs on crash | Low | `finally` block in orchestrator always deletes. Startup cleanup of dirs older than `maxTimeLimit + 1min`, skipping any submission ID currently in the in-memory active set. |
903	| Memory stats unavailable after removal | Low | `AutoRemove = false`; stream `GetContainerStatsAsync` until exit; prefer wrapper-side `memory.peak`. |
904	| EF migration fails mid-deploy | Low | Migration runs before `compose up`; non-zero exit halts deploy, old containers keep serving. |
905	| EF migration succeeds, app fails to start | Medium | Migration discipline (§10a): additive only; rollback procedure documented (restore backup + redeploy previous image). |
906	| Worker dies mid-job → stuck `Running` row | Low | `StuckSubmissionReaper` on startup transitions rows older than `maxTimeLimit + 1min` to `InternalError`. SIGTERM handler drains in flight or marks `InternalError`. |
907	| Worker hung → queue silently grows | Low | Worker writes `worker:heartbeat` to Redis every 5s (TTL 30s); `/health/ready` fails if stale. |
908	| SignalR falls back to long-poll | Low | `proxy_read_timeout 86400` on nginx; long-poll still works. |
909	| Both sandbox slots occupied | By design | Redis queue serializes work; jobs wait. |
910	| DooD requires `/var/run/docker.sock` | Low | Documented in §11; standard on any Docker host. |
911	| Postgres data loss on disk failure | Medium | Daily local backup + nightly offsite rsync (§11c); quarterly restore drill. |
912	| Disk filling from logs | Low | Docker `json-file` driver with `max-size: 10m, max-file: 3`; Seq retention 14 days. |
913	| HTTPS cert expiry | Low | Certbot sidecar renews every 12h; nginx reloads on cert change. Monitor cert age via `/health/ready` extension (future). |
914	| Abusive submission spam | Medium | Rate limiter on `/submissions` (5/min/user) and `/auth/*` (10/min/IP); GitHub OAuth raises the cost of throwaway accounts. |
915	
916	---
917	
918	## 15. Future Work (Post-v1)
919	
920	| Feature | Notes |
921	|---|---|
922	| Admin UI | Problem + test case + ProblemSet + Organization + Tag CRUD (currently DB-seeded only) |
923	| Per-test-case execution as separate containers | Tighter isolation; rejected for v1 due to ~200 ms overhead per case |
924	| Redis Streams queue | Upgrade from BLPOP list for consumer groups + replay |
925	| Horizontal worker scaling | Remove in-process semaphore; use Redis for distributed slot tracking |
926	| `WebIde.Tests` project | Currently no test project; CI `dotnet test` passes vacuously. Should backfill before scaling team. |
927	| Metrics / alerting | Prometheus + Grafana or hosted (Uptime Kuma, Better Stack). Currently logs-only via Seq. |
928	| Multi-region / HA | Single VPS = SPOF. Acceptable for v1; revisit when usage warrants. |
929	| Staging environment | Currently develop → main → prod. No environment to test deploy mechanics. |
930	| OAuth providers beyond GitHub | Google, GitLab as student access broadens |
931	| Editorial / hint system | Per-problem rich content beyond the markdown description |
932	
933	---
934	
935	## Appendix A — What Changed from Initial Plan
936	
937	This revision folded a gap-analysis pass focused on production-grade readiness. Highlights:
938	
939	- HTTPS moved from "future work" to Phase 0 / §11b — required for OAuth to function.
940	- DataProtection keys persisted to Redis (§10) — otherwise every deploy logs every user out.
941	- Redis `requirepass` added (§9a, §11) — was unprotected.
942	- Postgres/Redis/Seq port publication removed from compose; Seq behind nginx basic auth (§3, §11).
943	- Sandbox `--ulimit nproc` replaced with `--pids-limit`; `--user nobody:nogroup` added; image digest pinning (§6).
944	- Judging contract specified explicitly (§6a): output normalization, float tolerance, multi-case model, scoring, stdout caps.
945	- Deploy pipeline rewritten so `:latest` is promoted only after health-check pass; rollback uses captured digest, not `:latest` (§8).
946	- Migration discipline section added (§10a): additive-only rule + manual rollback procedure with backup restore.
947	- Postgres backup with offsite target added (§11c) + restore drill.
948	- Worker SIGTERM handling, heartbeat, stuck-submission reaper specified (§7 Phase 2, §10, §14).
949	- Rate limiter moved from future work to Phase 1 (§10).
950	- Antiforgery wiring for JSON POST specified (§10).
951	- nginx config expanded with `X-Forwarded-Proto`, rate-limit zones, gzip, Seq auth (§10).
952	- Adversarial verification suite added (§13b).
953	- Local dev quick-start added (§13a).
954	- Secret inventory enumerated (§9a).
955	- Tailwind moved from Play CDN to build-time compile (§7 Phase 0, §10).
956	- Phase numbering fixed; per-phase acceptance criteria added (§7).
957

# WebIde — Deployment Handoff Document

**Date:** 2026-06-06 (rev. 2026-06-06 — production-grade pass)
**Author:** Claude Code
**Status:** Plan approved — ready for implementation
**Branch:** develop → main

---

## 1. What This Document Is

A complete handoff specification for taking WebIde from a read-only local .NET app to a production-ready, auto-deploying competitive coding platform on Hetzner. Any engineer picking this up should be able to implement end-to-end from this document alone.

This revision treats the platform as **production-grade**: real users, hostile submissions, HTTPS required, persistent data, recoverable from a single-host failure.

---

## 2. Current State

| Area | Status |
|---|---|
| ASP.NET MVC web app | ✅ Working (read-only, no auth) |
| PostgreSQL + EF Core | ✅ Fully integrated, migrations exist |
| Tailwind CSS + Razor views | ✅ Brutalist design system applied (via Tailwind Play CDN — must be replaced, see §7 Phase 0) |
| Code execution | ❌ Data model exists, no implementation |
| Authentication | ❌ None |
| Docker / docker-compose | ❌ Not created |
| CI/CD | ❌ Not created |
| Nginx + HTTPS | ❌ Not created |

**Scope note for v1:** `ProblemSet`, `Organization`, and `Tag` entities exist in the model but ship as **read-only seeded data**. No admin UI in v1; managed via SQL until the admin-UI phase (§15).

---

## 3. Target Architecture

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │              Hetzner VPS                     │
                    │         (4 cores / 8 GB RAM)                 │
                    │                                              │
Internet ──HTTPS──▶ │  nginx :80/:443  (TLS termination, HSTS)     │
                    │      │                                       │
                    │      ├──/auth/* /submissions/* (rate limited)│
                    │      ├──/hubs/* (WebSocket upgrade)          │
                    │      └──/seq/* (basic-auth gated)            │
                    │      │                                       │
                    │      ▼                                       │
                    │  webide-app :8080  (ASP.NET MVC + SignalR)   │
                    │      │  ▲                                    │
                    │      │  │ pub/sub (SignalR backplane)        │
                    │   queue│ │                                   │
                    │      ▼  │                                    │
                    │  webide-worker (.NET Worker Service)         │
                    │      │                                       │
                    │      ▼ Docker socket (DooD)                  │
                    │  sandbox containers (sibling, ephemeral)     │
                    │      gcc / python / node                     │
                    │                                              │
                    │  postgres  redis  seq   (Docker net only)    │
                    │  (no published ports)                        │
                    └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### Why Docker-out-of-Docker (DooD)?
The worker mounts `/var/run/docker.sock` from the host so sandbox containers run as siblings (not children) on the host daemon. This avoids the `--privileged` requirement of Docker-in-Docker and is simpler to operate on a single-node deployment. The worker container itself never runs user code; sandbox containers have zero permissions (§6).

### Network exposure
- Only nginx publishes ports (80, 443) to the host.
- `postgres`, `redis`, `seq` listen on the internal `webide-net` Docker network only — **no `ports:` directive** in compose. Inspect Postgres with `docker compose exec postgres psql`; access Seq via `https://YOUR_DOMAIN/seq/` behind nginx basic auth.

---

## 4. Resource Allocation

| Service | CPU limit | RAM limit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| nginx | 0.1 | 64 MB | Reverse proxy + TLS |
| webide-app | 0.5 | 512 MB | ASP.NET MVC + SignalR |
| webide-worker | 0.3 | 256 MB | Manages containers, doesn't run code |
| postgres | 0.5 | 1 GB | Primary datastore (tune `shared_buffers=256MB`) |
| redis | 0.1 | 128 MB | Queue + sessions + SignalR backplane + DataProtection keys |
| seq | 0.3 | 512 MB | Log aggregation (14-day retention) |
| postgres-backup | 0.1 | 64 MB | Nightly `pg_dump`, rsync to offsite |
| **sandbox slot 1** | **0.9** | **512 MB** | Per-run limit, enforced by Docker |
| **sandbox slot 2** | **0.9** | **512 MB** | Max 2 concurrent at once |
| OS overhead | 0.3 | 512 MB | — |
| **Total** | **4.0** | **~4 GB** | Leaves ~4 GB headroom |

Sandbox CPU intentionally set to 0.9 (not 1.0) so the app/worker keep responsiveness under burst load — CFS throttling on the user-facing path is worse than slightly slower judging.

**Dynamic slot calculation** (for future server upgrades):
```
slots = min(
  floor((totalRam     - reservedRam) / sandboxMemMb),
  floor((totalCpu     - reservedCpu) / sandboxCpu)
)
```
Both bounds matter. RAM-only is wrong on small-core boxes; CPU-only is wrong on small-RAM boxes. Controlled by `Worker__MaxConcurrentSandboxes` env var (override if you want fewer slots than the formula allows).

## 5. End-to-End Submission Flow

```
1. User → GET /problems/{id}
         └─ Monaco editor (CDN), language picker, Submit button

2. User clicks Submit
         └─ fetch POST /submissions (JSON: problemId, language, sourceCode)
            with header RequestVerificationToken: <antiforgery>
         └─ Rate limiter: 5/min per user (429 on excess)
         └─ Antiforgery validated via IAntiforgery.ValidateRequestAsync
         └─ Submission record created (status = Pending)
         └─ SubmissionJob JSON pushed to Redis list "submissions:queue"
         └─ Response: { submissionId: 42 }

3. Browser opens SignalR connection
         └─ hub.invoke("SubscribeToSubmission", 42)
         └─ Status chip shows: PENDING (spinner)

4. Worker (BLPOP)
         └─ Deserializes SubmissionJob
         └─ Acquires SemaphoreSlim slot (blocks if both slots busy)
         └─ Updates Submission.Status → Running
         └─ Publishes Running event → browser shows: RUNNING

5. Worker spawns ONE sandbox container per submission
         └─ Writes source to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/solution.{ext}
         └─ Writes test cases to /tmp/webide-src/{id}/cases.json
         └─ docker run [see §6 for full flags]
         └─ Wrapper iterates cases internally (see §6a)
         └─ Wrapper exits with summary; per-case results in /tmp/result.json
         └─ Worker reads results from stdout (capped) + container exit

6. Container finishes (or is killed)
         └─ Worker collects per-case verdicts, wall time, peak memory
         └─ Evaluator computes final status + score (see §6a)
         └─ Writes ExecutionResult + updates Submission.Status
         └─ Publishes result to Redis channel "execution:42"

7. RedisSubscriptionService (IHostedService in app)
         └─ Receives from "execution:*" pattern subscription
         └─ Pushes to IHubContext<ExecutionHub>.Clients.Group("submission:42")

8. Browser receives ExecutionResult via SignalR
         └─ Status chip: ACCEPTED / WRONG_ANSWER / TIME_LIMIT / COMPILE_ERROR / RUNTIME_ERROR
         └─ Wall time + memory + per-case verdicts displayed
```

**Exit codes from sandbox wrapper (final result):**

| Exit | Meaning | Maps to status |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | All cases handled (per-case verdicts in JSON) | Computed from JSON: Accepted/WrongAnswer/TLE/MLE/RuntimeError |
| 2 | Compile failed (C/C++ only) | CompileError |
| 124 | Wrapper itself timed out (defense-in-depth) | TimeLimitExceeded |
| ≠ 0,2,124 | Wrapper crash | InternalError |

Note: Python and Node have no compile step. A `SyntaxError` at runtime exits 1 and is mapped to `RuntimeError`, **not** `CompileError`. This is intentional: there is no separate compile phase to fail in, so a parse error is a runtime error from the platform's perspective. UI should still surface the stderr so users see the syntax error.

## 6. Sandbox Security Model

Every sandbox container runs with these exact Docker flags — **non-negotiable**:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  --network none \              # zero network access
  --read-only \                 # root filesystem immutable
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=64m,mode=1777 \
  --memory $MEMORY_MB \         # min(problem.MemoryLimitKb/1024, 512)
  --memory-swap $MEMORY_MB \    # disable swap (no swap escape)
  --cpus 0.9 \                  # matches §4 slot allocation
  --pids-limit 64 \             # cgroup PIDs cap — defeats fork bombs
  --user nobody:nogroup \       # non-root inside container
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  --cap-drop ALL \              # drop all Linux capabilities
  --ulimit fsize=67108864 \     # 64 MB max file size (defense in depth)
  -v /tmp/webide-src/{id}:/code:ro \  # source + cases: read-only
  webide-sandbox-{lang}@sha256:{digest} \
  /code/solution.{ext} /code/cases.json
```

### Why `--pids-limit`, not `--ulimit nproc`
`--ulimit nproc` is a *per-UID rlimit*, applied at the host level. With containers sharing UIDs, nproc aggregates across containers and the host, so a fork bomb can exhaust the host's UID budget even when contained. `--pids-limit` is a *cgroup pids controller* — strictly per-container — and is the correct primitive.

### Why `--user nobody:nogroup`
Defense in depth. Even with `--cap-drop ALL` and `no-new-privileges`, root inside a namespace has more attack surface (mount, ptrace, specific syscalls). `nobody:nogroup` costs nothing and reduces it. Each sandbox Dockerfile must add `RUN adduser -D -H -u 65534 nobody 2>/dev/null || true && addgroup -S nogroup 2>/dev/null || true` (Alpine).

### Why pin image digests
`@sha256:{digest}` rather than `:latest` makes the sandbox behavior reproducible across deploys. Without it, a base-image refresh changes language minor versions silently and breaks judging determinism.

**What code CANNOT do:**
- Make network requests (outbound or inbound) — `--network none`
- Write to filesystem (except `/tmp`, capped 64 MB) — `--read-only`
- Fork more than 64 processes — `--pids-limit`
- Escalate privileges — `--cap-drop ALL` + `no-new-privileges`
- Read other submissions' source files — isolated host dir + read-only mount
- Communicate with any other container — no network
- Swap to disk — `--memory-swap` matches `--memory`

**C/C++ compile strategy:** A `compile-and-run.sh` wrapper inside the gcc image compiles to `/tmp/a.out` then runs it against each test case. Exit 2 = compile failure. For Python/Node, the wrapper directly invokes the interpreter per case.

---

## 6a. Judging Contract

Without this section, "WRONG_ANSWER" disputes will dominate the bug tracker. These rules are normative.

### Multi-test-case execution model
- One sandbox container per submission.
- Wrapper script reads `/code/cases.json` (array of `{ id, stdin, expected }`).
- For each case: spawn the user program as a subprocess, pipe `stdin`, capture stdout/stderr, time it, kill at `problem.TimeLimitMs + 500ms` grace.
- Wrapper writes `/tmp/result.json` with `[{ id, verdict, wallMs, peakKb, stdout, stderr }, …]`.
- Wrapper prints `result.json` to stdout (capped, see below) for the worker to ingest.

### Output normalization (applied before comparison)
1. Convert CRLF → LF.
2. Strip trailing whitespace on each line.
3. Strip trailing blank lines.
4. Internal whitespace preserved (do not collapse).

### Float comparison
- Per-problem flag `Problem.FloatTolerance` (double, nullable). If null → exact string match after normalization.
- If non-null → tokenize both expected and actual on whitespace; lengths must match; each pair compared via `|a - b| ≤ tol ∨ |a - b| ≤ tol × |b|` (combined absolute + relative).
- Schema change: add `double? FloatTolerance` to `Problem`. Default null. Migration: `AddFloatToleranceToProblem`.

### Per-case verdict precedence
For a single case: `MemoryLimitExceeded` > `TimeLimitExceeded` > `RuntimeError` > `WrongAnswer` > `Accepted`.

### Submission-level scoring
- `Submission.Score = Σ TestCase.Points` over accepted cases.
- `Submission.Status` is the worst per-case verdict by the precedence above. (`Accepted` only if every case is Accepted.)

### Stdout/stderr caps
- Per-case: 4 MB stdout, 1 MB stderr. Truncate with marker `\n[truncated: N bytes elided]\n`.
- Wrapper-level output to worker: 16 MB JSON max. Worker truncates `stdout`/`stderr` fields in DB to 64 KB.

### Wall time and memory measurement
- Wall time: measured by the wrapper around each user-process invocation (clock_gettime CLOCK_MONOTONIC), **not** Docker daemon timestamps for the whole container — that includes interpreter warm-up and is unfair.
- Peak memory: `getrusage(RUSAGE_CHILDREN).ru_maxrss` after each subprocess exit. On Linux this is in KB and is per-process; for multi-process programs use cgroup `memory.peak` from inside the container if available.

## 8. GitHub Actions Deployment Pipeline

### CI Gate (`.github/workflows/ci.yml`)
Triggers: push to `develop`, PR to `main`

```
checkout → setup-dotnet 10.x → dotnet restore → dotnet build → dotnet test
```

Branch protection rule on `main`: this workflow must pass before merge (see §12a).

### Sandbox Pipeline (`.github/workflows/sandbox.yml`)
Triggers: push to `main` where `sandbox/**` changed.

```
1. Login to ghcr.io
2. For each lang in [gcc, python, node]:
   - docker buildx build sandbox/{lang}.Dockerfile
   - Push ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/sandbox-{lang}:sha-{SHA}
3. Output digests as workflow artifact for deploy.yml consumption
```

Sandbox images are pinned by digest in deploy. They are **not** rebuilt on every deploy — only when their source changes.

### Deploy Pipeline (`.github/workflows/deploy.yml`)
Triggers: push to `main`

```
1. Login to ghcr.io
2. Capture rollback target:
     PREV_DIGEST=$(docker buildx imagetools inspect ghcr.io/.../app:latest --format '{{json .Manifest.Digest}}')
     # Saved for rollback even if :latest gets overwritten
3. Build + push app:sha-{SHA}, worker:sha-{SHA}
     (Do NOT push :latest yet.)
4. SSH into Hetzner (appleboy/ssh-action):
   a. cd /opt/webide
   b. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose pull webide-app webide-worker
   c. Run migrations (see §10a):
        docker run --rm --env-file .env --network webide_webide-net \
          ghcr.io/fbeleta/web_ide/app:sha-{SHA} \
          dotnet ef database update --no-build
   d. IMAGE_TAG=sha-{SHA} docker compose up -d webide-app webide-worker
   e. Poll GET https://localhost/health (30 attempts × 2s = 60s timeout)
        Also poll /health/ready which checks worker heartbeat.
   f. ✅ Pass:
        - From CI: re-tag and push ghcr.io/.../app:sha-{SHA} as :latest
        - docker image prune -f
   g. ❌ Fail:
        IMAGE_TAG=${PREV_DIGEST} docker compose up -d
        (Rolls back to the digest captured in step 2 — not :latest, which may have been overwritten.)
```

**Required GitHub Secrets:**

| Secret | Value |
|---|---|
| `HETZNER_HOST` | Server IP address |
| `HETZNER_USER` | SSH username (e.g. `deploy`) |
| `HETZNER_SSH_KEY` | Private SSH key (Ed25519 preferred) |

**Blue-green mechanism:** `IMAGE_TAG` env var controls which image Docker Compose uses. The `:latest` tag is promoted **only after** the health check confirms the new container is healthy. On failure, rollback uses the digest captured at step 2 (saved as `PREV_DIGEST`), guaranteeing we restore the previous known-good image even if step 3 had partially overwritten registry tags.

**Important:** The migration `docker run` must use `--network webide_webide-net` so it can reach the `postgres` service by hostname. Migrations follow the discipline in §10a — they must succeed against both the previous-version app and the new-version app to allow rollback after migration.
